module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
sublecture:
2.1.1 Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844 - Part 1
, Incomplete unit
content: Today we start with our second module starting with Marx, and then we will go on to Durkheim in this module. So for Marx?s, we'll study two excerpts from two of his major works. So first we will start with the economic and philosophical manuscripts of 1844. So Marx was considered the father of communism. He, in his time though, he was considered a political economist and a journalist and activist. So Marx has left a very important legacy. And today he is known for his impact in terms of the communist revolution that took place. However, his work as a political economist, as a sociologist that is what we'll focus on today. Because while we might disagree with his ideologies regarding communism, that is not what we are concerned with. We are concerned with understanding society and social structure. We are concerned with understanding specifically how as entrepreneurs an understanding of society and social structure will help us. So it is from that perspective that we will approach Marx. So we'll understand him not from an ideological point of view. We'll understand him as a theoretician in helping us to see how, which of his ideas are still relevant for us to understand society and social structure. So with that background in mind, let's first look a little bit about Karl Marxist early life. So he was born in Prussia. Today, there's nothing called Prussia. So Prussia at that time is basically present day Germany. He was born to Lutheran parents. Now keep in mind that he actually, his father was a, a Jew but he converted from Judaism to become a Lutheran to evade the antisemitism. So at that time, there was very strong antisemitism. So to evade that his family actually converted. Now Marx, he studied law at his father's request at Bonn University in Germany. Although his own interest was much more in philosophy and literature now at the University of Berlin where he soon, he, his basic instincts came to the fore and he felt very much at home when he joined a circle of at the time they were considered quite extreme thinkers who were challenging existing institutions and ideas. They were challenging religion, they were challenging philosophy, they were challenging ethics, politics, the economic system. It was a time of intellectual ferment. So Karl Marx found himself very much at home with these people. And you'll see a similar pattern in the works of Hobbes, of Adam Smith, of Rousseau sort of this questioning of the existing institutions including religion, ethics, politics, and economics and the same kind of trend we see in Marx's work. So among the many intellectuals that he came across, he was especially influenced by the work of Hegel who talked about how history progresses through dialectics. So there are two contradictory ideas, and the dialectic between these contradictory ideas leads to some kind of progress. So you'll see the same thing in Marx's work especially when in the next subsection where we discuss Marxist manifesto. So we will come back to this idea of dialectics and how different contradictory forces can lead to historical progression. Now after school, Marx's turn to writing and journalism to support himself, and he became the editor of a liberal newspaper where he expressed his intellectual dissent, and he published articles which were questioning the existing institutions. And so the Berlin government actually banned from publication. And so Marx given that he now no longer had a way to earn a living, he left Germany and he never returned and he moved to Paris and this is where he met his long time collaborator, Friedrich Engels. Now, Friedrich Engels story is also very interesting and we will see this a little bit more when we discuss the manifesto. Friedrich Engels was the son of a factory owner. So he was what Marx would call a capitalist. But Engels was Marx's biggest supporter throughout his life and in fact financially also, if it wasn't for Engels, Marx would probably never have been able to publish his work. So we can see the irony of that. Now, so he was living in France but he got chased out of France too. Because of the people in power opposed his ideas. And so Marx moved to Brussels and then London. So he really led this life of poverty life on the run where he was pursued and persecuted by various governments. While Engels became his lifelong friend and supported him financially throughout his journey while he was trying to write his book. And so he spent most of his life working on his manuscript thus capital which, and we will see excerpt from that in the next subsection. What about Marxist impact? So several revolutions occurred propelled by groups that adopted his ideas on communism. So including in Russia, in China, and flags and banners in China, in Russia for instance, depicted Vladimir Lenin, the leader of the Russian Revolution together with Marx. Similarly the case was in China where similar flags showing the leader of that country's revolution Mao Zedong together with Marx these were prominently displayed. So Marx, although he was basically a scholar, a writer, a journalist, his ideas really instigated several revolutions and changed perhaps the course of human history. Now to, before we get into Marx's ideas you know, let's think a little bit about the context. You know, last module we ended with Adam Smith and Adam Smith is talking just when the industrial revolution is starting you know, where the factory system is coming into play where you are dividing labor into little, little bits so that instead of producing whatever 100 nails, suddenly you are producing 10,000 nails, right. And so in Adam Smith's work, we see the excitement, the promise of this revolution that is happening. And Smith is talking about how division of labor industrialization will transform society, will make us prosperous, will make us all wealthy. And now we see Marx with a very, very different view. But keep in mind that Marx is about a 100 years after Smith. So while Smith was in the beginning of the industrial revolution and he was looking at this whole thing with so much of hope for human prosperity and human advancement, Marx has now seen after a 100 years what actually happened and that excitement of industrialization has faded, and now the consequences have become clear and Marx sees that it is not universal wealth as Smith imagined and hoped for but actually what was seen is extreme inequality and a quality of life, which one could argue, and that is what Marx argues that a quality of life for the worker, which is actually lower than what it was before. So this is what, this is the context in which Marx is talking. So without understanding that context then, you know, it is very difficult to understand how Smith and Marx are looking at actually the same thing. Division of labor industrialization but coming to dramatically different conclusions. So keeping that context in mind, let's now jump into the economic and philosophical manuscripts. So the economic and philosophical manuscripts are perhaps less well known than thus capital but without understanding economic and philosophical manuscripts, one can't actually understand thus capital because this is the foundational work which leads to the manifesto. Which we will read next. So now before we start to quickly summarize, what is Marx saying in the economic and philosophical manuscript? Marx is basically starting with a description of the condition of the worker in the modern capitalist system. So he makes this very eloquent case. And if you have a chance to read the whole excerpt, please do Marx writes you know, very beautiful language actually. And we will see some of those quotations we will see today in our own class. So Marx describes the worker being reduced to a commodity, just like we have salt and pepper, and there's no differentiation between one salt packet and another salt packet. Just like that he talks about how the worker is also a commodity with no differentiation between them. And not just that the worker is reduced to a mindless commodity but the worker is the poorest of all. As society's wealth grows, the worker's misery also grows. And this inequality becomes worse and worse, and society splits into two classes. You know, you don't have all those distinctions, which we had before of guild workers of merchant class none of those. Everything just splits into two classes, property owners and propertyless workers. So this is essentially the case that Marx is making in the economic and philosophical manuscripts. So I'll start with a quotation from Marx. Marx says "The worker sings to the level of a commodity and becomes indeed the most wretched of commodities that the wretchedness of the worker is an inverse proportion to the power and magnitude of his production that The necessary result of competition is the accumulation of capital in a few hands, and thus the restoration of monopoly in a more terrible form and that finally the distinction between capitalists and land rentier like that between the tiller of the soil and the factory worker disappears, and that the whole of society must fall apart into the two classes, the property owners and the propertyless workers". So that's end quote. So starting with that quote Marx then basically provides a critique of a political economy as a discipline. So what he says is that political economy assumes private property. It assumes private property as a fact which cannot be changed. So it doesn't try to explain private property, he just assumes it as a taken for granted fact. And so it explains wages, profit, rent but it ignores where all this is coming from. So it takes the interest of the capitalist, the property owner as the ultimate cause or objective. So according to Marx, the biggest criticism of political economy is that it takes for granted what it's supposed to explain. So Marx ends with his critique by saying that the only wields which political economy sets in motion are greed and the war amongst the greedy which is competition. So political economy according to Marx fails to explain the necessary inevitable and natural historical evolution from slavery to the feudal system, to the guild system, to a taxable third estate under monarchy to the modern capitalist system. So all of these are distinct economic, political systems that human history has gone through. Slavery, feudal system, guild system, taxable third estate under monarchy modern capitalist system. So Marxist critique is not just that political economy takes for granted what it is supposed to explain. It also doesn't explain why is it that this progression happened. So according to Marx, economists treat history as accidental or intentional change. But they do not see these as necessary outcomes of historical processes that are inevitable. Now, Marx says that we must grasp the intrinsic links between private property and avarice, the separation of labor, capital, landed property, the connection of change and competition of value, and the devaluation of men of monopoly and competition. We have to grasp this whole estrangement connected with the money system without which we cannot really understand what is happening. So Marx goes on to say how economists invents the invent, these fictional origins where they say barterly leads to exchange which leads to money and prosperity. So he criticizes them saying that they're like theologians explaining evil by the fall of man, you might have heard of Marx here is referring to Adam and Eve and you know, the eating of the apple and the fall of humans which is what led to the original sin and evil. So he's drawing an analogy between the political economists and the theologian. So he's criticizing both of them and saying that both are instead of explaining what they're supposed to explain, they assume it a way by some fictional fact. Yeah, so in the case of theologians, he is criticizing them by saying that instead of explaining evil which is what you're supposed to do. You're just inventing the story of the fall of man, of human to, to explain evil. So Marxist criticism is that such stories assume what they should explain. So Marx says this is, we cannot start with these kind of fictional stories. You cannot explain reality with fictional stories. We should start from real economic facts, not myths.
